message:
fix(packs/database/mongodb): close compound-command bypass in safe patterns + recognize method-call destructive forms

Two related hardening fixes for the mongodb pack.

1. Prefix-trigger expansion
---------------------------
`prefix_triggers` previously listed only bare shell command names
(`mongo`, `mongosh`, `dropDatabase`, `dropCollection`, `deleteMany`).
An invocation like:

  mongosh 'mongodb://host/db' --eval 'db.users.drop()'

does hit the `mongo*` CLI trigger, but a raw one-liner like:

  bash -c 'db.users.drop()' | somepipe

did not match any trigger and the pack was never consulted. Add
the method-call forms and the data-movement CLIs to the trigger
list:
  .drop(
  .remove(
  .deleteMany(
  mongorestore
  mongodump

`mongorestore --drop` is specifically dangerous because it drops
target collections before restore; `mongodump` is added for
completeness so the pack runs its safe/destructive patterns on
both halves of a backup/restore workflow.

2. Compound-command bypass in safe patterns
-------------------------------------------
Safe patterns (`mongo-find`, `mongo-count`, etc.) previously
matched on the presence of `.find(` / `.count(` anywhere in the
command. That whitelisted:

  mongosh --eval 'db.users.drop(); db.posts.find({})'
  mongosh --eval 'db.posts.find({}); dropDatabase'
  mongosh --eval 'db.posts.find({}).then(() => db.users.drop())'

because the safe `find(` token matched first. The destructive
branch never ran.

Fix: prepend a negative-lookahead forbid clause to every safe
pattern. The forbid clause rejects the match if the command string
contains any destructive Mongo method:

  dropDatabase
  dropCollection
  .drop(
  .remove({}) / .deleteMany({})  (the no-filter forms, which
                                  delete everything)
  mongorestore --drop

Each safe pattern now looks like:
  ^(?!.*(?:<forbid-set>)).*\.find\s*\(

The whole-string lookahead ensures the safe match only holds when
no destructive Mongo verb appears anywhere in the command. The
destructive patterns below are unchanged — they still match on
their own verbs; this commit just stops the safe patterns from
shadowing them.

Net effect: the seven compound-command cases the new test harness
would flag now correctly route to the destructive branch and
trigger user confirmation, instead of silently exiting 0.
